Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine at all. I'd tell myself I was going to study, but then I'd just... not. Sometimes I'd sit down with my laptop open and end up scrolling through stuff or watching videos instead. When I did study, it was usually late at night when the anxiety got bad enough that I couldn't ignore things anymore. I know I spent about 22 hours a week supposedly studying, but a lot of that time was just me being in front of my materials without actually absorbing anything.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then suddenly panic when I realized something was due soon. Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving, and even then I'd often miss them anyway. My extracurriculars sometimes gave me an excuse not to study, which I know sounds bad but it's true. I never had a set schedule that I actually stuck to.
Q3: Usually it was guilt or panic that made me log in. I'd get a notification or remember that I hadn't checked in for a while, and the anxiety would spike enough to make me open the LMS. Sometimes I logged in just to make myself feel like I was doing something, even if I didn't actually engage with anything meaningful. Other times I'd see classmates posting in discussions and feel like I should at least show up.
Q4: For a little while, maybe in the first few weeks, I did get into a habit of checking the LMS in the mornings. But that faded pretty quickly when I started feeling overwhelmed and sad about everything. The mindfulness exercises they had us do actually helped me feel a bit better, but they didn't really translate into better study habits. It never became something I did automatically like checking social media.
Q5: The video lectures were honestly the most useful for me because I learn better by listening. I could put them on and actually absorb some information without having to read through dense text. The discussion boards were okay too because sometimes hearing other students' perspectives helped things click. I watched a lot of video content, probably more than most people, but I'm not sure how much of it actually stuck with me.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the reading materials and the practice problems. Reading just made me zone out, and the practice problems felt overwhelming when I was already behind. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never came. The optional stuff was basically invisible to me because I could barely handle the required things. I also avoided anything that felt too complicated or time-consuming.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it didn't require too much mental energy. Like, watching a video felt manageable, but writing a long response felt exhausting. If something seemed like it would take forever or I didn't understand the point, I'd just skip it. I needed things to feel achievable, which wasn't often given how behind I always felt.
Q8: Optional activities basically didn't exist in my mind. I was already struggling to complete the required stuff, so anything labeled optional was automatically put in the 'never going to happen' category. Required activities at least had some external pressure attached to them, even if that pressure mostly just made me anxious. The optional stuff felt like a luxury for students who had their lives together, which wasn't me.
Q9: Yeah, sometimes I'd download lecture slides or PDFs and then study from those on my phone or printed copies. I liked being able to highlight things or listen to recordings while doing other stuff. There were also times I'd ask friends to explain concepts to me instead of going through the official materials. A lot of my learning happened through conversations or just thinking about things while I was walking around.
Q10: There were definitely times when I was processing course content mentally but not doing anything on the LMS. Like, I'd think about lecture topics while lying in bed unable to sleep because of anxiety. Or I'd discuss assignments with classmates in person or over text, which helped me understand things better. None of that would show up anywhere official, but it was still learning in a way.
Q11: All the time, actually. I'd download a video or save some notes, watch or read them once, and then keep coming back to those files offline. It felt easier than logging into the LMS every time, especially when my internet was being slow or I just didn't want to deal with the platform. The LMS wouldn't know I was reviewing that stuff repeatedly on my own device.
Q12: Things got really hard around midterms when everything piled up at once. I was already feeling sad and unmotivated, and then the workload just crushed me. My attendance dropped because I couldn't bring myself to go to class, and the more I missed, the harder it was to catch up. The anxiety made it worse because I'd avoid looking at what I'd missed, which only made the hole deeper.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or stressed about other things, the LMS was the last place I wanted to be. My extracurriculars took up energy, and sometimes family stuff or just feeling emotionally drained made studying impossible. I'd open the LMS, see all the things I hadn't done, feel overwhelmed, and close it again. It became this cycle where the platform reminded me of my failures.
Q14: So many times. I'd be logged in, watching a video or scrolling through materials, but my mind was completely elsewhere. I was going through the motions without actually learning anything. Sometimes I'd finish a video and realize I couldn't remember a single thing from it. It was like my body was present but my brain had checked out.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially assignments. I knew they were important, but the thought of starting them made me anxious, so I'd avoid them. It's like this weird thing where knowing something matters makes it scarier to attempt because you might fail. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come and I'd say the same thing. My assignment completion rate shows exactly how bad this got.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me do anything, but even then I often missed them. I'd have this burst of panicked activity right before something was due, logging in frantically and trying to throw something together. But if the deadline felt too close and I was too far behind, I'd just give up entirely. It was all or nothing with me and deadlines.
Q17: Feedback didn't really change much for me. When I actually submitted something and got good feedback, like my assignment scores were pretty high when I did them, it felt nice for a moment but didn't motivate me to do more. And when feedback was critical, it just made me feel worse about myself. I think I was too caught up in my own head to really use feedback constructively.
Q18: The structure felt overwhelming to me. There was so much content and so many deadlines that I couldn't figure out where to start. I needed more hand-holding, I think, or smaller chunks that felt manageable. The way everything was laid out made it easy to fall behind and hard to catch up. I kept getting lost in what I was supposed to be doing and when.
Q19: Having videos helped because of how I learn, but the sheer amount of reading and written assignments made things harder. I wish there had been more audio options or interactive stuff that didn't require so much reading. Also, the discussion posts felt forced sometimes, like I was just posting to meet a requirement rather than actually engaging. The mindfulness intervention actually helped my mood a lot, but it came too late to save my grades.
Q20: I think that was literally me. I was on the LMS a lot, watching videos, reading discussions, but I couldn't bring myself to actually submit work. It's like there's a huge gap between consuming content and producing something. The anxiety of being judged on my work was paralyzing, so I'd engage with materials but freeze when it came to assignments. High activity doesn't mean you're actually completing things.
Q21: I can see how that happens if someone is really efficient or studies mostly offline. That wasn't really me though—I had high LMS activity but still failed because I wasn't submitting work. But I guess some people can absorb everything quickly and don't need to spend hours on the platform. Everyone learns differently, and the LMS doesn't capture all the ways people study.
Q22: Logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you were actually paying attention or learning anything. They miss all the offline studying, the conversations with classmates, the mental processing that happens away from the screen. They also can't capture why someone isn't engaging—like whether it's laziness or anxiety or depression or just life being overwhelming. Numbers don't tell the whole story.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting effort into understanding it. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials—it's being mentally present and invested. I wanted to be engaged, I really did, but my motivation was so low and my anxiety was so high that I couldn't make it happen. True engagement requires your head and heart to be in it, not just your login credentials.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was struggling emotionally throughout the course. It wouldn't show the nights I spent crying because I felt like a failure, or the panic attacks before deadlines, or how the mindfulness exercises actually helped me feel less terrible even if they didn't fix my grades. It also wouldn't show that I actually understood more than my exam scores suggest—I just couldn't perform under pressure. My mental state was the biggest factor in my performance, and none of that shows up in data.
